items.q: What is the worst-case complexity of bubble sort?
items.misconception: O(n) because it just passes through the list.
items.why: Students count one pass and forget that bubble sort does up to n passes, each comparing up to n elements. The nested loop structure gives n times n = n squared.

items.correct: The ability of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.
items.misconception: Having multiple classes with the same name.
items.random_wrong: Variables that change type at runtime.
items.why: Students confuse polymorphism with naming. Polymorphism means 'many forms' - a single interface (method signature) behaving differently depending on the object's actual class.

items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.
The answer examiners want is: L1. “L2” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which factor most helps a task that cannot be split into parallel work?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Clock speed. “Number of cores” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A 3.5 GHz CPU runs how many cycles per second, in billions?
items.why: Exact answer: 3.5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
